coredata.dc:title: Energy-optimized microchannel liquid cooling for 21700 lithium-ion battery packs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Bidwaik, Akash S.
Bhusnoor, Siddappa S.
Nikam, Shailesh R.
coredata.dc:description: Effective thermal management of high-power lithium-ion batteries is critical for safety, stable performance, and long service life in electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems. This study presents a novel microchannel liquid-cooling system for 21700-type cylindrical lithium-ion cells, integrating numerical simulations with experimental validation to achieve efficient thermal control. The system incorporates optimized microchannel geometry and low-flow cooling strategies to enhance convective heat transfer while minimizing energy consumption. Thermal performance was evaluated at Reynolds numbers from 400 to 700 and coolant inlet temperatures of 20–30°C. Increasing the Reynolds number improved heat dissipation by up to 15%, reducing the maximum temperature gradient from 2.45°C to 1.86°C while maintaining a uniform surface temperature as low as 27.3°C at a 20°C coolant inlet. Notably, the optimized configuration achieved up to 75% lower coolant flow rates than conventional designs, with a maximum pumping power of only 0.145 W, representing less than 0.1% of the 180 W battery output. These findings demonstrate that the proposed system delivers high thermal uniformity and significant energy efficiency, highlighting its strong potential for scalable integration into compact, thermally robust battery packs for electric mobility and grid-scale applications.

head.ce:title: Energy-optimized microchannel liquid cooling for 21700 lithium-ion battery packs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective thermal management of high-power lithium-ion batteries is critical for safety, stable performance, and long service life in electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems. This study presents a novel microchannel liquid-cooling system for 21700-type cylindrical lithium-ion cells, integrating numerical simulations with experimental validation to achieve efficient thermal control. The system incorporates optimized microchannel geometry and low-flow cooling strategies to enhance convective heat transfer while minimizing energy consumption. Thermal performance was evaluated at Reynolds numbers from 400 to 700 and coolant inlet temperatures of 20–30°C. Increasing the Reynolds number improved heat dissipation by up to 15%, reducing the maximum temperature gradient from 2.45°C to 1.86°C while maintaining a uniform surface temperature as low as 27.3°C at a 20°C coolant inlet. Notably, the optimized configuration achieved up to 75% lower coolant flow rates than conventional designs, with a maximum pumping power of only 0.145 W, representing less than 0.1% of the 180 W battery output. These findings demonstrate that the proposed system delivers high thermal uniformity and significant energy efficiency, highlighting its strong potential for scalable integration into compact, thermally robust battery packs for electric mobility and grid-scale applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The transportation sector relies heavily on fossil fuels, which significantly contribute to deteriorating air quality. This reliance also intensifies the progression of global climate change. This pressing environmental challenge highlights the critical need to transition toward sustainable mobility solutions. Notably, the adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) offers a cleaner and more climate-resilient alternative [1]. Among different energy storage methods, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are primarily used in EVs due to their long cycle life, high energy density, and low self-discharge. However, these batteries generate considerable heat during charging and discharging, primarily due to internal resistance and exothermic electrochemical reactions. The resulting temperature increase can lead to thermal runaway, posing serious safety risks, including the potential for fire or explosion. To ensure operational safety and extend battery lifespan, an effective battery thermal management system (BTMS) is crucial for regulating cell temperatures within an optimal range of 15–45°C. Additionally, the maximum allowable temperature difference across the battery pack (ΔTmax) should not exceed 5°C to ensure even thermal distribution and optimal performance [2]. Various cooling methods have been developed for BTMS, including air cooling, phase change materials (PCMs), and liquid cooling. Air and PCM-based systems are often limited by their low thermal conductivity, which results in inefficient heat dissipation and decreases their suitability for high-performance electric vehicles.

In contrast, liquid cooling provides enhanced heat transfer capability, largely attributed to the high specific heat capacity of the working fluid. Owing to their effectiveness in maintaining optimal thermal conditions, liquid-cooled BTMS have become increasingly prevalent in commercial electric vehicles. Leading OEMs such as Tesla, BMW, and Tata Motors have implemented these systems in models like the Model 3, iX, and Nexon EV, respectively, to extend battery life and ensure performance stability under varying operational scenarios [3]. Liquid cooling configurations are generally categorized into two primary methods: direct and indirect. Direct systems, though efficient, come with inherent risks such as fluid leakage, electrical short circuits, and higher energy losses [4]. On the other hand, indirect liquid cooling, typically implemented via liquid-cooling plates (LCPs), provides enhanced safety and thermal management by physically separating the coolant from the battery cells. LCP systems have attracted significant research interest due to their effectiveness in achieving uniform thermal distribution within battery packs. Consequently, many studies have focused on structural design, flow dynamics, and integration strategies to improve BTMS performance, as discussed below.

Zhou et al. (Zhou et al., 2024) employed numerical simulation with a stepped-channel liquid-cooled thermal management system, achieving a Tmax increase of only 2.52K with a 54.08% weight reduction, and an improvement in ΔTmax is implied by optimized design parameters. Zhu et al. [5] designed a 1D/3D electrochemical-thermal coupled numerical simulation with an orthogonal design of experiments, achieving a Tmax reduction of 0.74°C and a ΔTmax improvement of 0.18°C with a rib-grooved liquid-cooled plate compared to the conventional design. Wu et al. [6] employed a validated 3D numerical model alongside experimental testing to assess a 60-cell immersion-cooled BTMS, achieving a peak temperature of 34.22°C and maintaining ΔTmax below 5°C at flow rates >0.5 L/min, with a simulation–experiment deviation under 0.43°C. Subramanian et al. [7]conducted validated ANSYS Fluent simulations to evaluate a liquid-cooled BTMS with aluminum fins, reporting a peak temperature of 40°C and ΔTmax under 5°C. Ethylene glycol outperformed water at a flow rate of 3 ms-1. Mubashir et al. [8] employed experimental and numerical investigations to evaluate the thermal performance of a double serpentine channel (DSC) cold plate compared to a single serpentine channel (SSC) cold plate, achieving a Tmax reduction of 6.4% and a ΔTmax reduction of 41%, with an 86.7% decrease in power consumption at a flow rate of 120 mL/min. [9] utilized an orthogonal experimental approach combined with NSGA-II optimization to design and analyze a tree-structured channel cold plate, achieving a Tmax reduction of 13.94% and a ΔTmax reduction of 52.94% compared to a conventional straight-through cold plate, with superior heat dissipation and pressure drop performance. Hongkun et al. [10] employed experimental and numerical investigations to analyze a novel battery liquid-cooling system incorporating a ⊥-shaped oscillating heat pipe (OHP) under pulsating flow conditions, achieving a Tmax reduction of 9.8% and a ΔTmax reduction of 1.3°C (17.8%), with improved surface temperature uniformity and stable OHP operation. Q. Gao et al. [11] utilized a Multi-Scale Multi-Domain electrochemical-thermal model validated through simulation and experiments to analyze a liquid immersion battery cooling system (LIBCS) with fish-shaped flow guides, achieving a Tmax reduction of 12.2% and maintaining a ΔTmax below 5°C, with improved cooling performance and reduced pump power consumption. R. Kumar & Goel [12] experimentally evaluated the performance of an oscillating heat pipe (OHP) using acetone, DI water, methanol, and ethanol as working fluids at various filling ratios and ambient temperatures, achieving a Tmax reduction of 17% (to 39.4°C) and a ΔTmax reduction of 65% (to 3.5°C) compared to a bare battery cell. Kanbur et al. [13] experimentally analyzed the thermal performance of 3D-printed cold plates with body-centered cubic (BCC) and pillar elements under varying coolant inlet temperatures and air velocities, achieving a Tmax maintained below limits at 15.7°C inlet temperature and a ΔTmax improvement with performance evaluation criterion values ranging from 1.2 to 2.4, outperforming traditional designs. Z. Wu et al. [14] employed theoretical calculations, experiments, and simulations to design a passive thermal management system for eVTOL batteries, achieving a Tmax of 38.46°C and a ΔTmax of 3.85°C. Z. Li et al. [15] combined numerical and experimental analyses to optimize an immersion thermal management system for lithium-ion battery modules, achieving a Tmax of 15.5°C and a ΔTmax of 1.18°C. Rana et al. [16] experimentally investigated a liquid-based BTMS using various working fluids, flow configurations, and discharge rates, achieving a Tmax reduction of 17.1°C and a ΔTmax of 1.5–2.4°C with nanofluid (0.45% ϕv MWCNTs) in configuration III. Wei et al. [17] developed and optimized a liquid cooling thermal management system for EV battery packs using simulations, experiments, and multi-objective genetic algorithms, achieving a Tmax reduction of 27% and a ΔTmax of 2°C. Saha et al. (Saha et al., 2024) introduced an LCP design with dual inlets and a single outlet, achieving a peak battery temperature of 36°C at a coolant velocity of 0.06 ms-1 and a discharge ΔT of 5°C. Chen et al. [18] designed a biomimetic double-layered LCP inspired by leaf-vein architecture, achieving up to 90% lower power consumption than traditional serpentine channels for lithium-ion battery cooling. Kausthubharam et al. [19] examined the thermal characteristics of mini-channel LCPs for pouch-type lithium-ion batteries, reporting a 75% enhancement in temperature uniformity and a reduction in maximum temperature of approximately 30% relative to optimal operational thresholds observed in prior designs. Rabiei [20] numerically evaluated a foam-enhanced liquid-cooled BTMS, demonstrating a 4% reduction in battery temperature and a 99% cut in pumping power across varied climatic conditions. Wang et al. [21] employed a butterfly-shaped channel design in the LCP for prismatic battery modules, demonstrating enhanced thermal performance with a reduction in maximum temperature of up to 29% compared to conventional configurations. Yang [22] numerically analyzed a cross-linked LCP design, showing a 13% reduction in pumping power relative to conventional layouts, with no loss in thermal efficiency. Chen et al. [18] compared hexagonal and diamond LCP geometries, finding that the hexagonal design increased pumping power by 21%, while fork-fin integration reduced peak battery temperature by 12 K. Bidwaik et al. [23] performed a 3D numerical analysis of a microchannel liquid-cooled BTMS, revealing that increasing Re from 400 to 700 reduced battery temperature (from 38.69°C to 34.25°C) and ΔTmax (from 2.45°C to 1.86°C), improving thermal performance at the cost of higher pumping power. Talha and Palange [24] employed a comprehensive analytical review and modeling approach to evaluate advanced thermal management strategies, particularly a hybrid energy storage system (HESS) integrating metal hydrides and phase change materials. The outcomes reveal that this hybrid system enhances heat dissipation, improves temperature uniformity by up to 30%, and effectively mitigates thermal runaway, ensuring safer and more energy-efficient EV battery performance. Kumar et al. [25] used COMSOL simulations and infrared thermography to test a liquid-cooled copper-tube system for EV batteries, achieving a 20% drop in peak temperature and better thermal uniformity for safer, longer battery life. Akash S. Bidwaik, Bhusnoor, and Nikam [26] used a 3D numerical analysis of a liquid-cooled microchannel BTMS for cylindrical 21,700 lithium-ion batteries, showing that circular microchannels with cross-flow achieve the best performance, reducing maximum temperature to 30.2°C, improving temperature uniformity by ∼20%, and enhancing thermo-hydraulic performance by 2–4%.Jangid [27] used SIMULINK and ANSYS FLUENT simulations to test PCM cooling in EV batteries, showing RT 44 HC cuts temperatures by up to 38K for safer, more efficient performance. Ali et al. [28] developed a Metaheuristic Structure Binary-Distribution (MSB) method combining metaheuristic search and gradient descent for thermal structure optimization. Results show superior weight reduction and lower thermal compliance, with 0% material segregation verified through successful 3D printing. Al and Masatoshi [29] employed a multiphysics multiscale topology optimization framework integrating conduction, convection, and radiation to design porous thermal actuators. Results show a 45% performance boost and 75% weight reduction, enhancing thermal efficiency and structural resilience. Ali et al. [30] applied the Metaheuristic Aided Structural Topology Optimization (MASTO) method to design heat sinks with optimal thermal conductivity and low EMI. Results show enhanced heat dissipation and effective EMI suppression, confirming the method’s practicality for complex multiphysics designs. Zhu et al. [31] developed a transient model of a stepped TEG using half-Heusler and bismuth-telluride modules that boost output and efficiency, achieving 32.3% higher power (276.6 W) and 5.9% efficiency over conventional designs. Wang et al. [32] designed a self-powered wireless sensor network (WSN) using flexible thermoelectric generators (FTEGs) with integrated cooling fins for vehicle monitoring. Experiments show 1760 μW output power and 37.9% efficiency, enabling accurate, maintenance-free real-time sensing. Zhang et al. [31] developed a TCN-BiLSTM neural model integrated with an equivalent circuit model (ECM) to generate and analyze lithium battery fault data. Results show accurate early detection of internal short-circuit faults, ensuring reliable battery safety diagnostics. Gao et al. [33] reviewed droplet impact dynamics and heat transfer mechanisms in spray cooling through statistical and interaction-based analyses. Findings reveal how droplet behavior and film conditions govern cooling efficiency under high heat flux conditions. Zhu, Zuo, Wang, Jia, et al. [34] used A stepped TEG with high-, medium-, and low-temperature modules that was optimized using RSM and TOPSIS, achieving up to 7.589% efficiency and 515.02W power, with a comprehensive performance factor of 0.923, confirming enhanced overall performance. Wang and Wang [35] developed a Quantum-Classical Hybrid Genetic Evolutionary Algorithm-Based Topology Optimization Framework QCHGEA-TOF, which quantum-based parallel global search with classical FEA and BESO refinement, achieving approximately 8–15% lower structural compliance than conventional GA and BESO methods. Z. Wang et al. [36] proposed a global information gain-based multi-sensor placement optimization integrated with a ParallelGraphNet (parallel graph construction and multi-scale pooling GCN), achieving fault diagnosis accuracy exceeding 95% and significantly outperforming conventional single-sensor and standard GCN approaches. Liu et al. [37] reviewed surrogate model-assisted multi-objective optimization techniques and quantitatively compared transfer learning, gradient sampling-based multi-fidelity, and search space decay surrogate models, demonstrating improved optimization accuracy and robustness in a five-phase PMSM design with reduced computational cost compared to conventional physical modeling approaches. Yin et al. [38] employed finite element (FE) modeling and curve-fitting–based predictive modeling to analyze and optimize chiplet drop reliability, achieving a 98.4% reduction in deformation at the critical location and a 39.9% reduction in maximum stress on the Si interposer through structural design optimization. K. Wang, Wang, and Li [39] proposed a swarm intelligence–based structural topology optimization method for cooling channels, validated against commercial numerical software, demonstrating effective optimization of channel spacing and flow distribution with improved cost-effectiveness and thermal performance compared to conventional approaches.

This study addresses a critical, yet underexplored, aspect of battery thermal management: optimizing heat dissipation in cylindrical battery cells. Unlike prismatic and pouch cells, which are well-suited for LCPs due to their flat surfaces, cylindrical cells pose unique thermal challenges due to their curved geometry and limited contact area. These constraints necessitate innovative, space-efficient cooling strategies. Prior studies have primarily concentrated on the thermal and hydraulic performance of both minichannel and macrochannel configurations. At the same time, the potential of microchannels, despite their superior surface area-to-volume ratio and enhanced heat transfer characteristics [40], remains untapped mainly in the context of BTMS. To overcome current thermal management challenges, this study introduces an advanced microchannel-based liquid-cooling architecture specifically designed for cylindrical lithium-ion cells, providing a compact, high-efficiency solution that enhances thermal regulation and operational safety in battery modules.

Although the classification of microchannels, minichannels, and macrochannels remains a subject of ongoing debate due to inconsistencies across various studies, Mehendafe et al. [41] introduced a widely accepted channel classification methodology that segments flow passages based on their characteristic dimension (CD ). Their taxonomy includes microchannels (1–100 μm), minichannels (100 μm–1 mm), compact channels (1–6 mm), and conventional channels (>6 mm). This dimensional breakdown also accounts for differences in fabrication techniques, flow physics, and surface-to-volume ratios, all of which are critical to thermal system design.

In response to the limitations of earlier classification models, Kandlikar and Grande [42] proposed a refined categorization of flow channels that accounts for gas rarefaction and microscale transport behavior. Using the hydraulic diameter (Dh ) as the defining parameter, the framework classifies flow passages into four categories: transitional microchannels (1–10 μm), microchannels (10–200 μm), minichannels (200 μm–3 mm), and conventional channels (>3 mm). This updated scheme offers enhanced guidance for the design and analysis of microscale thermal–fluid systems, particularly in emerging energy storage and heat exchanger technologies.
To address limitations in previous classification schemes, Obot [43] proposed a hydraulic diameter–based method that defines microchannels as flow conduits with Dh < 1 mm.

The integration of microchannels into the BTMS represents a transformative approach to thermal regulation, particularly for high-power-density lithium-ion battery packs. By leveraging their high surface area-to-volume ratio, microchannels significantly enhance convective heat transfer between the coolant and the battery casing. This geometric configuration promotes efficient thermal energy extraction from the battery surface, reducing thermal resistance and maintaining cell temperatures within safe operating limits. As a result, the system ensures uniform heat dissipation and suppresses thermal gradients across battery modules. This mitigates the risk of thermal runaway and is critical for improving the electrochemical stability, safety, and longevity of the battery system.

Beyond thermal efficiency, this microchannel architecture eliminates bulky external cooling components, reducing overall system weight and structural complexity. Such compact integration supports space-constrained applications, particularly in EVs and portable energy storage devices, where form factor is a limiting constraint. Additionally, the reduced thermal mass and targeted heat-removal strategy enable lower coolant volumes and simplified flow distribution networks, thereby improving energy efficiency and reducing pumping power requirements. These advantages collectively lead to a cost-effective, lightweight, and scalable BTMS that aligns with the stringent economic and performance benchmarks set by next-generation energy storage platforms for e-mobility and grid-scale applications.

A comprehensive three-dimensional numerical simulation model and bench-scale experimental setup were developed in this study to investigate the thermal performance of the proposed microchannel-cooled BTMS.

The methodology adopted in this study aims to systematically achieve the objectives of developing and evaluating the performance of a BTMS. The approach integrates theoretical, numerical, and experimental studies to ensure a comprehensive understanding and validation of the system’s performance. Initially, a theoretical analysis is conducted to determine the overall design parameters. These parameters help define the operating conditions and are further used to estimate performance parameters. In the numerical phase, the microchannel heat exchanger is modeled and simulated using ANSYS Fluent to analyze fluid flow and heat transfer characteristics under various operating conditions. The experimental studies involve developing a test setup, defining an experimentation matrix, and conducting controlled experiments to validate numerical results.

A computer-aided design (CAD) model of the BTMS was developed, excluding electrical interconnects and auxiliary components to focus on thermal behavior. The geometric model shown in Fig. 1 depicts a microchannel-assisted liquid-cooling configuration designed to manage thermal loads within a BTMS comprising ten 21700-type cylindrical lithium-ion batteries (C1–C10). Table 1 outlines the key electrochemical and thermal characteristics of the cells. The battery pack geometry measures 137.4 mm × 71 mm × 57.8 mm (length × height × width). To enhance convective heat transfer, the aluminum casing of the battery pack is equipped with 60 circular microchannels arranged in three vertical columns, each containing 20 channels with a Dh of 1 mm. This configuration promotes uniform and direct coolant distribution around the lithium-ion cells, increasing the effective surface-area-to-volume ratio while limiting pressure drop to acceptable levels, in accordance with established microchannel cooling design principles.

To develop the analytical model, several foundational assumptions were made. A steady-state flow condition was assumed throughout the system, and the thermophysical properties of the coolant, battery, and enclosure were assumed to be spatially uniform, as detailed in Table 2 . The flow characteristics within the microchannels were quantified using the Reynolds number (Re), a key dimensionless parameter that distinguishes between laminar and turbulent flow regimes. Re serves as an indicator of the relative dominance of inertial forces over viscous forces, and is calculated to assess the likelihood of flow regime transition, as shown in Equation (1) (Qi et al., 2024).

Given the constant cross-sectional geometry, the coolant’s mass flow rate is directly proportional to its mean velocity, with ρ, v, μ, and Dh denoting the fluid density, flow velocity, dynamic viscosity, and hydraulic diameter, respectively. In this study, water is employed as the working fluid due to its favourable thermophysical properties, and the microchannel geometry features a diameter of 1 mm. For circular cross-sections, the hydraulic diameter simplifies to the actual diameter, as expressed in Equation (2).[44].
(2)

In this context, A denotes the cross-sectional flow area of the microchannel, and P denotes the wetted perimeter that interfaces with the coolant.

According to the fluid properties of water at 25°C provided in Table 2, a Re of 400 corresponds to a coolant velocity of 0.357 ms-1 for a microchannel with a Dh of 1 mm. To evaluate thermal-fluid performance under varying thermal conditions, the same Re was used to estimate corresponding velocities at different ambient temperatures. At 20°C, the required velocity increases to 0.402 ms-1 due to higher dynamic viscosity, whereas at 30°C, it decreases to 0.320 ms-1 owing to reduced viscous resistance. These values confirm that the flow remains in the laminar regime across the investigated temperature range, supporting stable, predictable heat transfer characteristics, which are crucial for efficient battery thermal management. This study focuses on the laminar flow regime, specifically for Re values between 400 and 700, to evaluate convective heat transfer performance in a microchannel-based thermal management system. The evaluation employs the Nusselt number (Nu) correlation formulated by Stephan and Preußer [45]), which is expressed as Equation (3). Although this correlation was originally developed for circular conduits and based on the dimensionless thermal entrance length (L*), it has been validated for use in a variety of non-circular and microscale geometries. Applying this correlation enables the determination of the convective heat transfer coefficient on the channel side, which is subsequently quantified using Equation (6) to support accurate thermal predictions in the microchannel domain.

Bernardi et al. [49] proposed a thermal model to evaluate the rate of heat generation within a single electrochemical cell under operational conditions. The model accounts for both ohmic losses and entropic contributions, expressed as in Equation (8).

where Q is the heat generated per unit volume, Vb is the cell volume, I is the current, Ri represents internal resistance, T is the absolute temperature, and dUo/dT denotes the temperature dependence of the open-circuit voltage. The thermal model simplifies the energy balance by neglecting radiation, which is assumed to have minimal impact compared to conduction and convection.

In the present numerical analysis, liquid water is selected as the working fluid for thermal regulation. The thermophysical properties of the coolant are denoted as follows: density ρcw , specific heat capacity Cpcw , temperature Tcw , velocity v, dynamic viscosity μcw , static pressure Pcw , and thermal conductivity kcw . At the inlet of the microchannels, a coolant flow velocity of 0.357 ms⁻1 is imposed with a specified temperature of 25 °C, whereas the outlet boundary condition is defined at standard atmospheric pressure. Various properties of the coolant vary with temperature, affecting flow velocity requirements: 0.402 ms⁻1 is necessary at 20°C to offset higher viscosity, while at 30°C, reduced viscosity allows operation at 0.320 ms⁻1. The inlet coolant temperature range (20–30°C) reflects typical battery operating environments and coolant supply conditions in EV thermal management systems. Under a 2C discharge rate, the volumetric heat generation within each cell is estimated to be 123,736 Wm⁻3. The selected 2C discharge rate represents a high-power operating condition commonly encountered in electric vehicle applications, thereby ensuring evaluation under thermally demanding scenarios. The corresponding volumetric heat generation rate was calculated based on the electrochemical energy balance under this discharge regime. A no-slip condition is enforced at all solid–fluid interfaces. These thermal and flow conditions are designed to emulate realistic operating environments, thereby enabling high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations for evaluating the BTMS’s thermal performance.

To evaluate the thermal performance of the BTMS, transient simulations were conducted in ANSYS Fluent 2023 R1 over 600 seconds, ensuring adequate time for the battery pack to reach thermal stabilization under transient discharge conditions. A pressure-based solver utilizing a second-order implicit discretization scheme was implemented to enhance both numerical stability and solution accuracy. The working fluid was treated as incompressible, and the flow field was modeled under laminar flow assumptions based on the system's Reynolds number. To accurately model environmental heat transfer, natural convection boundaries were defined on the outer walls of the aluminum casing, capturing passive cooling under ambient conditions.

To assess mesh sensitivity, a mesh independence study was performed, as illustrated in Fig. 2 , which presents the relationship between battery surface temperature and the total number of computational cells. Initial mesh refinements resulted in a notable decrease in the predicted surface temperature, from 38°C to 34.25°C, indicating that coarser grids were insufficient to capture temperature gradients accurately. Further refinements, as shown in Fig. 3 , produced diminishing changes in surface temperature, validating mesh convergence. A hybrid meshing strategy was employed, with hexahedral elements in the coolant domain to enhance numerical accuracy and tetrahedral elements in the battery pack to accommodate its complex geometry. The minimum orthogonal quality values were recorded as 0.44 for solid regions, 0.80 for the fluid domain, and 0.20 for the battery pack, all of which satisfy the quality criteria for reliable CFD analysis. The mesh independence test revealed negligible differences in temperature predictions: 0.87% between 6.13 × 10⁶ and 9.03 × 10⁶ nodes, and 0.15% between 9.03 × 10⁶ and 11.9 × 10⁶ nodes. These results confirm that additional refinement beyond 11.9 × 10⁶ elements offers limited improvement in accuracy. A computational mesh comprising approximately 11.9 million nodes was employed to achieve an optimal balance between numerical accuracy and computational cost. Pressure–velocity coupling was handled using the Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations (SIMPLE) algorithm, while second-order upwind schemes were applied for momentum discretization to enhance solution fidelity. A residual convergence criterion of 10⁻⁶ was applied to ensure numerical stability and accurate resolution of the coupled thermal–fluid interactions. Steady-state convergence was achieved after 650 iterations. For transient simulations, a time step of 1 s with 5 inner iterations per step was employed, determined through a temporal sensitivity analysis to ensure an optimal balance between numerical accuracy and computational efficiency. The results of the mesh sensitivity analysis demonstrated that the chosen computational grid and solver configuration were sufficiently refined to capture the thermal characteristics of the microchannel-integrated liquid-cooling system with high precision.

A bench-scale experimental setup, illustrated in Fig. 4 , was developed to evaluate the thermal performance of a microchannel-based liquid-cooled BTMS integrated with a vapor compression refrigeration (VCR) cycle. The battery cooling system comprises two coolant storage tanks, each with a capacity of 20 L. Tank 01 stores chilled coolant, while Tank 02 collects the heated fluid exiting the battery pack before recirculation. The VCR cycle comprises a 0.5 TR compressor, a finned-tube condenser, a capillary tube as the expansion device, and a helical coil evaporator submerged in Tank 01. A 12 V DC pump circulates coolant through microchannels embedded in the battery casing. The microchannel heat exchanger was selected for its high thermal efficiency, compact size, and low resistance. A flow control valve (FCV) regulates the mass flow rate in response to the thermal load. Battery heating and charging conditions are simulated using a 210 W, 36 V resistive heater and a 42 V, 2 A charging unit. Voltage and current are monitored via a digital DC ammeter and voltmeter. Pressure readings at the coolant inlet (P1) and outlet (P2) are obtained using a digital transmitter. Temperature measurements at the exact locations (Tc1 and Tc2) are acquired using K-type thermocouples. All sensors are connected to a digital temperature indicator. The coolant flow rate is monitored using a rotameter. As heat is absorbed, the coolant exits the battery pack at a higher temperature and flows into Tank 02. It is then recirculated to Tank 01, completing the closed-loop cycle, as shown in Fig. 4.

The battery casing, illustrated in Fig. 5 , is fabricated from aluminum to enhance thermal conductivity and promote effective heat dissipation. It encloses cylindrical 21700 lithium-ion cells to form a compact battery module with the real-time thermal monitoring system, as shown in Fig. 6 . The fully assembled bench-scale experimental setup is shown in Fig. 7 . To capture axial temperature gradients, temperature sensors are strategically positioned 24 mm above and below each cylindrical cell. This configuration facilitates accurate mapping of temperature distribution and enables the identification of localized thermal hotspots. Sensor outputs are transmitted to a digital display unit for continuous data acquisition. The system's closed-loop design enables uninterrupted thermal monitoring, which is crucial for evaluating electrochemical stability and mitigating thermal runaway risks, especially under high-power operating conditions. Furthermore, the setup enables high-resolution thermal mapping, which is essential for correlating experimental measurements with numerical simulation results and validating the thermal model.

This setup ensures consistent thermal regulation, preventing overheating and enhancing battery performance and lifespan. The experimental platform offers a reliable system for testing advanced BTMS strategies under real-world conditions and verifying their thermal efficiency.

The experimental study aims to assess the thermal performance of a BTMS integrated with a microchannel heat exchanger under controlled operating conditions. A fully charged 36V battery pack is initially subjected to a controlled discharge at a 2C rate using a resistive heater as the load, allowing the battery surface temperature to reach a predefined threshold. Once the desired temperature is reached, coolant circulation is initiated to regulate the battery temperature. Initially, coolant at ambient temperature is stored in the coolant storage tank 01, where it is cooled to the required setpoint using a VCR system. The coolant is then pumped through an FCV to regulate the flow rate before entering the integrated microchannel casing of the battery pack. The coolant extracts heat from the battery pack, increasing in temperature as it exits through the coolant outlet, where its temperature (Tc2) and pressure (P2) are recorded. The heated coolant is collected in storage tank 02, from where it is subsequently recirculated back to tank 01 for re-cooling, forming a closed-loop thermal management cycle. This process is repeated for different coolant inlet temperatures (Tc1) to analyze the system’s cooling efficiency and the microchannel heat exchanger's heat dissipation characteristics. Throughout the experiment, battery surface temperatures, as well as the inlet and outlet coolant pressures (P1, P2) and temperatures (Tc1, Tc2), are continuously monitored and recorded. This experimental approach ensures precise thermal regulation, mitigates the risk of thermal runaway, and optimizes battery longevity. The integration of a microchannel heat exchanger significantly enhances heat transfer efficiency, ensuring effective thermal dissipation, particularly in high-power-density battery systems. The results of this study provide valuable insights into optimizing cooling strategies for next-generation battery technologies.

The quantification and analysis of experimental errors constitute a critical component of rigorous scientific investigation, as such errors can undermine the integrity of research outcomes by introducing discrepancies between observed and true values (Shown in Table 3 ). These deviations impact key performance metrics and compromise measurement reliability. Error sources are multifaceted, encompassing factors such as instrumentation calibration, material inconsistencies, environmental variability, operator-induced uncertainties, limitations in experimental design, and challenges in data interpretation. In metrology and empirical research, an experimental error is formally defined as the difference between a measured value and a recognized reference or true value. This deviation serves as a direct indicator of measurement uncertainty, underscoring the necessity for comprehensive uncertainty analysis to strengthen the validity and reproducibility of experimental results. Among the standard techniques for estimating cumulative error, the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) is widely used because it captures the aggregated magnitude of deviations across datasets [53]. The RMSE formula, which estimates the total error propagation in a given function, is expressed as follows:

This study systematically investigates the thermal behavior of a microchannel-enhanced liquid-cooled BTMS, supported by detailed numerical and experimental correlation discussed in subsequent subsections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive thermo-hydraulic investigation of liquid-cooled battery thermal management system
ce:section.ce:para: Comparing numerical and experimental results for heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop in microchannel-based BTMS provides important insights into model accuracy and system performance. As shown in Fig. 8a , the heat transfer coefficient increases almost linearly with Re. This trend reflects enhanced convective heat transfer at higher flow rates, driven by improved fluid mixing and reduced thermal resistance. The relative error between numerical and experimental results decreases with increasing Re, indicating improved predictive accuracy of the numerical model. This reduction in error can be interpreted physically by considering the flow regime. At lower Re, microscale phenomena such as entrance effects, axial conduction, and developing flow conditions become more significant. These effects are highly sensitive to geometric imperfections and measurement uncertainties, making precise modeling more challenging. As the Re increases, the flow becomes more hydrodynamically developed and convection-dominated, reducing the relative contribution of these microscale effects and thereby improving agreement between numerical and experimental results. For pressure drop, both numerical and experimental results show a rising trend with Re. As illustrated in Fig. 8b , the numerical values closely match experimental data across most of the flow range. The increase in pressure drop with Re is fundamentally governed by enhanced wall shear stress. As fluid velocity increases, the velocity gradient near the wall becomes steeper, leading to greater viscous friction. Since pressure drop in internal flows scales approximately with the square of velocity in transitional and turbulent regimes, the nonlinear growth behavior observed in Fig. 8b is physically consistent with classical fluid dynamics principles. A minor deviation appears at higher Re, likely due to experimental uncertainties, surface roughness, or assumptions in numerical modeling, such as boundary conditions and turbulence modeling. The relative error in pressure drop remains within 1–1.25%, which is acceptable for engineering applications. The small magnitude of this deviation further indicates that the dominant momentum transport mechanisms are accurately captured by the numerical framework. In particular, the model accurately captures the balance between inertial forces and viscous dissipation, which primarily governs hydraulic resistance in microchannel systems. The overall reduction in error for both parameters with increasing Re confirms the validity of the numerical approach. From a physical standpoint, this convergence suggests that the governing conservation equations for mass, momentum, and energy are sufficiently resolved under convection-dominated conditions. The numerical model, therefore, provides a reliable and efficient tool for predicting thermal and hydraulic performance in microchannel BTMS, thereby reducing reliance on extensive experimental testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermo-electrochemical correlation of voltage drop and heat generation in lithium-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 provides a comprehensive representation of the interdependence between voltage and surface temperature as a function of battery capacity. As the battery discharges from 100% to 0% capacity, the terminal voltage declines from approximately 4.0 V to below 2.8 V. This progressive voltage reduction reflects the thermodynamic and kinetic limitations of lithium-ion intercalation. As discharge proceeds, the lithium concentration in the anode decreases while it increases in the cathode, altering the chemical potential difference between the electrodes. Consequently, the open-circuit voltage decreases according to the Nernst equation, while the internal resistance increases due to reduced ionic mobility and concentration polarization within the electrolyte. In parallel, the battery surface temperature increases from approximately 26°C to nearly 48.6°C, indicating substantial internal heat generation. The temperature rise is largely attributable to Joule heating, exothermic side reactions, and increased entropy at high discharge rates. Notably, the voltage and temperature curves intersect near 50% capacity, indicating a critical threshold at which thermal effects begin to dominate electrical behavior. The rapid voltage drop beyond 90% discharge suggests an increased rate of internal polarization and possibly the onset of irreversible degradation. At high depth of discharge, lithium concentration gradients within the electrodes become steeper, intensifying concentration polarization and limiting diffusion. This mass-transport limitation contributes to sharp voltage decay and elevated local heat generation, which can promote structural stress in electrode materials. The observed thermal trends underscore the importance of maintaining temperatures within safe operational limits, as excessive heat can facilitate degradation pathways, such as solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) dissolution and lithium plating, thereby compromising the structural and electrochemical integrity of the cell.

Fig. 10 illustrates the variation in the average battery temperature (Tavg) of a LIB module under different loading conditions (50%, 75%, and 100%) in the absence of a cooling medium over a 45-minute time duration. The temperature rise is plotted against time, illustrating the thermal response of the LIB module under increasing load intensities. At 50% loading, the initial temperature is approximately 30°C, and it gradually increases to around 38°C at the 45-minute mark. At a loading of less than 75%, the temperature follows a steeper trajectory, rising from 30°C to nearly 41°C. The 100% loading condition exhibits the largest thermal rise, starting at 30°C and reaching 48.5°C after 45 minutes. This direct correlation between higher loading percentages and increased temperature is attributed to greater internal resistive heating, governed by Joule heating (I2R losses). At higher loading levels, the current draw generates more heat, which accumulates because there is no active cooling mechanism. In the first 10 minutes, a rapid temperature increase is observed across all loading conditions, with the 100% loading condition reaching nearly 37°C, while the 75% and 50% loading conditions record approximately 35°C and 33°C, respectively. This steep early-stage rise reflects the dominance of internal heat generation over external heat dissipation. At the beginning of discharge, the temperature gradient between the battery surface and ambient air is relatively small, limiting natural convection and radiative heat loss. As a result, the net energy balance is strongly positive, causing a rapid increase in internal energy and temperature. As time progresses, the temperature curves exhibit a less steep slope, suggesting a transition towards a quasi-steady state in which heat dissipation through natural convection and radiation becomes more significant.

The absence of a cooling medium limits heat dissipation, increasing the risk of thermal runaway at higher loading conditions. Excessive temperature rise accelerates capacity fade, electrolyte decomposition, and electrode material degradation, making thermal management critical to ensuring LIB longevity and safety. Fig. 10 underscores the need for advanced cooling strategies to prevent performance deterioration and potential hazards in high-power LIB applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of coolant inlet temperature and flow condition on battery cooling performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 presents the temperature distribution in a microchannel liquid-cooled battery module for Re = 400–700 and inlet coolant temperatures (Tci = 20°C, 25°C, and 30°C). At a constant Tci, an increase in Re leads to a more uniform temperature distribution across the battery cells. This improvement in thermal uniformity results from enhanced convective heat transfer driven by increased fluid velocity, which thins both the hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layers along the microchannel walls. This increased velocity reduces the thermal boundary layer thickness, promoting better heat removal from the cell surfaces. At Tci = 20 °C, cells exhibit the lowest surface temperatures, as evidenced by the dominance of green and light-blue contours. As the inlet temperature rises to 25°C and 30°C, the contours shift towards yellow-orange, indicating higher average surface temperatures. However, the effect of increasing Re remains significant across all inlet conditions, highlighting the importance of flow rate control in BTMS design. The simulation demonstrates that lower inlet coolant temperatures and higher Re synergistically minimize thermal hotspots and enhance cell temperature uniformity. This balance is crucial for maintaining electrochemical stability and preventing thermal runaway in high-power battery systems, particularly during rapid charging or discharging.

Fig. 12 illustrates the dependency of the average battery surface temperature on the Re for three distinct Tci: 20°C, 25°C, and 30°C. Across all conditions, a clear inverse relationship between Re and surface temperature is observed, indicating improved convective heat transfer efficiency at elevated flow rates and at Tci 20 °C, and increasing the Re from 400 to 700 results in a significant reduction in the average battery surface temperature from approximately 31.9°C to 27.1°C, yielding a total decrease of 4.8°C. When the Tci is set to 25°C, the corresponding surface temperature decreases from 38.5°C to 35.3°C, reflecting a 3.2°C reduction. In contrast, for a higher Tci of 30°C, the surface temperature drops more modestly, from 42.1°C to 40.3°C, resulting in a decline of only 1.8°C. These results underscore two significant thermal management insights: (i) Increasing the Re leads to thinner thermal boundary layers and enhanced convective cooling, which is evident from the consistent temperature reduction trend. (ii) Tci substantially influences the system's thermal gradient, where lower Tci values amplify the temperature differential between the coolant and battery surface, thereby improving heat extraction efficiency. Moreover, the diminishing temperature drop at higher Tci values suggests a reduction in thermal driving potential, highlighting the importance of maintaining a sufficient temperature gradient to sustain effective thermal regulation. These findings collectively indicate that optimizing both Re and Tci is essential for maximizing the thermal performance of BTMS, particularly in high-discharge-rate lithium-ion battery applications.

Fig. 13 demonstrates the influence of Re on the ΔTmax within a battery pack for different Tci. As Re increases from 400 to 700, ΔTmax decreases consistently, indicating improved thermal management. At Tci=30°C, ΔTmax drops from approximately 3°C at Re 400 to around 2.4°C at Re 700, indicating a significant reduction in temperature gradients. Similarly, for Tci = 25 °C, ΔTmax decreases from about 2.4°C to nearly 2°C, while at Tci = 20°C, it declines from roughly 2°C to 1.7°C. This trend highlights the role of increasing Re in enhancing convective heat transfer, as higher flow velocities promote better cooling and thermal uniformity. The results underscore the importance of optimizing coolant flow rates in battery thermal management systems to minimize temperature differentials, prevent localized overheating, and enhance battery performance. A lower temperature gradient ensures uniform heat dissipation, reducing thermal stress and improving battery lifespan. Thus, increasing Re enhances cooling efficiency, making it a crucial design parameter for improving the reliability and safety of battery packs in electric vehicles and energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transient thermal response of battery surface under varying reynolds numbers and coolant temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 illustrates the transient response of the average battery surface temperature at Re = 400, 500, 600, and 700 and three coolant inlet temperatures (Tci = 20°C, 25°C, and 30°C). The results demonstrated that increasing Re enhanced convective cooling, resulting in lower steady-state battery temperatures across all scenarios. Increasing the Re increases coolant velocity, thereby strengthening momentum transport and reducing the thermal boundary layer thickness along the battery surface. This enhances the convective heat transfer coefficient and accelerates the removal of heat generated internally within the battery module. In Fig. 14a (Tci = 20°C), the initial battery surface temperature was uniformly 49°C. As the Re increased, the cooling rate improved due to a larger temperature gradient between the battery surface and the coolant. The system reached steady-state temperatures of 32.6°C (Re = 400), 31.5°C (Re = 500), 28.4°C (Re = 600), and 27.3°C (Re = 700) after 5, 4, 4, and 3 minutes, respectively. The faster approach to steady state at higher Reynolds numbers indicates a reduction in the system’s thermal time constant. This occurs because stronger convection increases the rate of energy extraction relative to the battery’s thermal storage capacity, thereby shortening the transient cooling period.

This trend confirmed that a higher Re promoted greater convective heat transfer efficiency. Fig. 14b (Tci = 25°C) showed a similar trend, although with slightly reduced cooling performance. The battery surface reached steady-state temperatures of 39.8°C, 38.5°C, 37.2°C, and 36°C for Re = 400, 500, 600, and 700, respectively, all after 4 minutes. The reduced temperature gradient accounted for the diminished cooling efficiency relative to the 20°C case. In Fig. 14c (Tci = 30°C), the battery again started at 49°C. The temperature declined rapidly in the initial minutes due to convective cooling but stabilized at higher values: 43.5°C (Re = 400), 41.8°C (Re = 500), 41.0°C (Re = 600), and 40.5°C (Re = 700), all of which occurred after 5 minutes. The smaller temperature gradient at this inlet condition reduced heat dissipation, resulting in elevated equilibrium temperatures. At this higher inlet temperature, the system approaches a quasi-equilibrium state in which the rate of internal heat generation within the battery balances the coolant's convective heat removal capacity. The elevated steady-state temperatures, therefore, reflect a reduced ability of the cooling system to offset internal Joule heating under diminished thermal driving force conditions.

Across all cases, a lower coolant inlet temperature (Tci = 20°C) consistently yielded the most effective thermal management. At lower Tci, this gradient was larger, thereby enhancing heat removal. Moreover, while increasing Re improved cooling performance, diminishing returns were observed at higher values, particularly beyond Re = 600. This was likely due to boundary layer effects and increased thermal resistance, which limited further gains in convective heat transfer. From a practical standpoint, maintaining a coolant inlet temperature near 20°C and operating at a Re in the range of 600–700 offered an optimal balance between cooling efficiency and system energy consumption. Such a strategy is crucial for effective battery thermal management, thereby improving performance, extending service life, and enhancing safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of pumping power consumption in a microchannel-based battery cooling system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 shows the variation in pumping power with Re for the microchannel-based BTMS. As Re increases from 400 to 700, the required pumping power rises from approximately 0.045W to 0.145W, reflecting the greater pressure drop and fluidic resistance associated with higher coolant velocities. As the Re increases, the coolant velocity rises, leading to higher wall shear stress and increased viscous dissipation within the microchannels. This enhanced momentum loss manifests as a larger pressure gradient that the pump must overcome, thereby increasing the mechanical energy input required. This trend aligns with fundamental fluid-dynamics principles: an elevated Re indicates increased inertial forces and, consequently, a higher energy input required to maintain the flow. Even though the system operates within the laminar regime, the pressure drop still increases approximately linearly with velocity due to frictional resistance along the channel walls. The narrow hydraulic diameter of microchannels amplifies surface friction effects, which explains the sensitivity of pumping power to changes in Re.

Despite the increase, the pumping power remains extremely low compared to the total battery power output of 180W used in the present study. Specifically, the maximum pumping power of 0.145W corresponds to only 0.08% of the total battery power, an exceptionally small parasitic load. Even at peak flow conditions (Re = 700), over 99.9% of the battery energy remains available for propulsion or system operation, underscoring the energy efficiency of the proposed cooling design.

This low power demand is a direct result of the microchannel configuration, which offers a high surface area-to-volume ratio, promoting effective heat transfer at modest flow rates. Furthermore, the system operates entirely within the laminar flow regime, minimizing turbulent losses and enhancing flow predictability. Collectively, these factors confirm that the microchannel-integrated BTMS is a highly efficient, low-energy thermal management solution, ideally suited for compact, high-performance battery systems, such as those in electric vehicles and aerospace applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative evaluation of current results with existing literature
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 16 illustrates a comparative evaluation of the influence of coolant channel flow rate on the maximum temperature (Tmax) of lithium-ion battery packs across various microchannel-based BTMS designs. The analysis encompasses both the present experimental and numerical results and prior literature [54,55,56,57,58], all conducted at a fixed coolant inlet temperature of 25°C. In the present study, battery packs achieve Tmax between 36.2°C and 39.8°C at flow rates ranging from 3.5×104 to 2.2×103 kgs-1, which are significantly lower than those reported in prior studies, which reported similar or even higher flow rates. For example, Gao, Fan, and Liu [54] and Tang et al. [59] maintain a temperature of approximately 40–44°C while employing coolant flow rates above 0.01 kgs-1. This indicates an overall reduction in coolant mass flow rate of approximately 75% in the present design compared to prior systems, which achieve similar or inferior cooling performance. This substantial reduction is attributed to optimized microchannel geometry, improved thermal contact resistance, and enhanced flow distribution in the current system. From a physical perspective, this means reduced pumping power requirements and improved system efficiency without compromising thermal safety limits. The data collectively confirms that effective thermal regulation in battery packs can be achieved at significantly lower coolant flow rates, emphasizing the importance of geometrical and system-level BTMS design optimizations over brute-force flow increases.

This study presents a comprehensive experimental and numerical evaluation of a novel microchannel-integrated liquid-cooling system for high-power lithium-ion battery thermal management in EV applications. The results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves high thermal uniformity, exceptional energy efficiency, and reduced coolant flow requirements, offering a compact and scalable solution for next-generation BTMS.

ce:para.ce:bold: High Thermal Uniformity with Low Energy Consumption:

ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized microchannel configuration maintains a maximum temperature difference (ΔTmax) below 2°C while requiring coolant circulation power of less than 0.1% of total battery output. This represents a significant advancement over conventional high-flow liquid-cooling systems, enabling compact, lightweight, and energy-efficient BTMS designs for high-power EV applications.
•
Optimized Flow–Temperature Coupling for Enhanced Heat Transfer:

Parametric analysis reveals that the synergistic combination of lower coolant inlet temperatures (20°C) and higher Reynolds numbers (Re = 700) significantly improves convective heat transfer, reducing average cell temperature by up to 4.8°C (≈15%). These findings provide actionable design guidelines for adaptive thermal control strategies in smart EV battery packs.
•
Benchmark-Level Cooling with Reduced Flow Demand:

The proposed system achieves comparable or superior cooling performance relative to reported BTMS designs while operating at up to 75% lower flow rates. This reduction in hydraulic demand directly contributes to improved system efficiency, lower auxiliary power consumption, and simplified integration into compact EV battery modules.
•
Minimal Pumping Power and High Integration Potential:

With a pumping power of only 0.145W at Re = 700 (0.08% of a 180 W battery output), the system demonstrates outstanding thermohydraulic efficiency. This ultra-low parasitic load makes the design particularly suitable for electric vehicles, hybrid mobility platforms, aerospace energy storage, and other applications where energy efficiency, safety, and weight reduction are critical.

ce:para.ce:bold: Practical Applications and Future Research Directions:

ce:section.ce:para: The proposed microchannel-integrated liquid cooling system demonstrates strong potential for practical deployment in high-energy-density electric vehicle battery packs, particularly in fast-charging platforms, compact modular battery architectures, and heavy-duty or high-performance EV applications where thermal uniformity, energy efficiency, and weight reduction are critical. Its low pumping power requirement and reduced coolant flow demand make it especially suitable for next-generation mobility systems aiming to maximize driving range and minimize auxiliary energy losses, while also being applicable in aerospace power systems and stationary energy storage installations that require reliable, compact thermal management. Future research should focus on full-scale battery pack validation under real-world operating conditions, incorporating electrochemical–thermal–mechanical multi-physics coupling to evaluate degradation behavior and long-term reliability. Additionally, exploring advanced heat transfer media, such as nanofluids and hybrid PCM–microchannel configurations, along with topology-optimized channel geometries, could further enhance thermal performance without increasing system complexity. The development of intelligent, adaptive flow control algorithms that integrate real-time thermal feedback and digital twin models represents another promising direction to improve operational efficiency and safety under dynamic load profiles. These advancements will help translate current laboratory-scale findings into scalable, commercially viable, and smart battery thermal management solutions that support sustainable electrification.

Akash S. Bidwaik: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Siddappa S. Bhusnoor: Visualization, Supervision. Shailesh R. Nikam: Visualization, Supervision, Data curation.
